feedingProfile.preferredNpk: balanced (e.g. 10-10-10), side-dress when vines spread / female flowers appear

commonIssues: Heavy water feeder: needs at least ~25mm (1 inch) water per week; soil dries faster in containers, so container plants may need daily watering in hot weather.
Very frost-sensitive warm-season annual: do not plant out until after last frost and soil is at least ~18C (65-70F) at seed depth.
Susceptible to squash vine borer, squash bug, and striped/spotted cucumber beetles.
Prone to powdery mildew and cucurbit viruses; keep foliage dry by watering at the base (drip/soaker), not overhead.
Poor fruit set often reflects a pollination problem (low pollinator activity), not a watering or feeding fault; early flowers are male only.
Container minimum of ~19L (5 gal) is the practical floor; a 38-57L (10-15 gal) container gives far better yield and far less watering stress.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing summer squash and zucchini in home gardens. Sow after last frost once soil is at least 70F at 2-inch depth (late May-early June in MN); vine crops need about one inch of water per week; keep foliage dry with drip/soaker hoses; side-dress when vines begin spreading (e.g. 1 cup 27-3-3 per 100 ft of row); best soil pH 6.0-6.5; pests include cucumber beetles, squash vine borer, squash bugs; diseases include powdery mildew and cucurbit viruses.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing summer squash and zucchini in home gardens. Slightly acidic best soil pH 6.0-6.5; side-dress fertilizer when vines begin spreading; soil test before planting (medium nutrient requirement).

University of Maryland Extension — Growing Summer Squash (Zucchini) in a Home Garden. Full sun requires direct light at least 6 hours/day; prefers 8-10 hours/day. Water deeply and regularly at the base, especially in hot dry weather and once fruits form. Medium nutrient requirement; incorporate compost/fertilizer before planting, then side-dress when female flowers appear. Very tender warm-season annual; frost will injure top growth. Days to maturity 50-65 from transplant. Spacing: bush types ~2-3 ft.

University of Maryland Extension — Growing Summer Squash (Zucchini) in a Home Garden. Very tender warm-season annual; frost will injure top growth; needs warm weather to grow. Pests/diseases: bacterial wilt, cucumber beetle, squash bug, squash vine borer, powdery mildew, downy mildew, pollination problems.

The Old Farmer's Almanac — Growing Zucchini: How to Plant, Grow, and Harvest. Direct sow ~2 weeks after last frost when soil is around 65-70F; needs 6 to 8 hours of direct sun daily; needs at least an inch of water each week; slightly acidic to neutral pH ~5.8-7.0; most varieties average ~60 days to maturity; harvest fruit at 6-8 inches.

Container sizing for zucchini is not given a single authoritative number by US extension services; the schema requires a container minimum. Synthesis of horticultural guidance places the practical floor near 5 gal (~19L) with 10-15 gal (~38-57L) markedly better given zucchini's large root system and high water demand. recommendedMinLiters, idealMinLiters, and idealMaxLiters are conservative estimates, not directly extension-sourced.

Estimated thermal thresholds: coldHardyMinC (~10C) reflects that zucchini is a very tender warm-season crop needing soil at least ~18C (65-70F) to germinate and warm air to grow (UMN, UMD, Almanac); heatStressC (~32C) reflects typical cucurbit heat/pollination stress. Exact stress temperatures are not published per-degree by these sources and are conservative estimates.
